medications.indication_rationale: First-line for uncomplicated AOE once otoscopy confirms an intact tympanic membrane. Combines antibacterial cover (incl. Pseudomonas) with anti-inflammatory to reduce canal oedema and pain. Source specifies 3 drops to the affected ear twice daily for 7 days.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 3 drops to the affected ear twice daily for 7 days (as per source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Must confirm intact TM before instillation — cannot be verified via chat; defer to in-person otoscopy

medications.indication_rationale: Adjunct analgesia for severe otalgia refractory to paracetamol alone; source advises 400mg every 8 hours PRN alongside continued paracetamol. No contraindication reported (NKDA, no GI/renal history volunteered).
medications.dose_route_frequency: 400mg orally every 8 hours as needed (as per source note)

medications.drug_name: aminoglycoside-only ototopical drops (e.g. framycetin/gentamicin) prescribed remotely
medications.indication_rationale: Aminoglycoside ear drops are ototoxic if the tympanic membrane is perforated. Prescribing any ototopical drop remotely without otoscopic confirmation of an intact TM is unsafe — the telehealth-correct step is same-day in-person otoscopy first.

medications.drug_name: oral antibiotics (e.g. amoxicillin/flucloxacillin) for uncomplicated AOE
medications.indication_rationale: Systemic antibiotics are not indicated for uncomplicated AOE (topical therapy achieves far higher local concentrations); reserved for spreading cellulitis or necrotising OE.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the ear dry — use a petroleum-jelly-coated cotton ball at the meatus when showering; do not swim until healed.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Canal moisture perpetuates infection and impairs epithelial healing.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not insert cotton buds or instruments into the canal.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Mechanical trauma re-introduces bacteria and delays healing.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like an infection of the ear canal (outer ear). It needs someone to look inside your ear with a scope today, because the ear drops that treat it can only be used safely once we know the eardrum isn't perforated.
Keep the ear completely dry and don't put cotton buds in it — moisture and trauma keep the infection going.
After drops are started, lie on the opposite side for a few minutes so they soak in. Pain should ease within 48–72 hours.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Pain, discharge, canal oedema and hearing; swab if not improving.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Spreading redness/swelling behind the ear, facial weakness, high fever, or worsening despite drops — seek urgent/emergency care.

must_include_items: Arrange same-day in-person otoscopy rather than prescribing ototopical drops blindly
Screen for necrotising-OE red flags (diabetes/immunosuppression, mastoid spread, facial weakness)
acceptable_alternatives: Acetic acid ± steroid ear drops or a topical quinolone-steroid combination are both acceptable once TM confirmed intact
Same-day urgent care or pharmacist-assisted GP review acceptable equivalents to GP otoscopy
errors_of_omission: No red-flag screen for necrotising OE
errors_of_commission: Prescribing aminoglycoside ear drops remotely without excluding TM perforation
Prescribing oral antibiotics for uncomplicated AOE
Reassuring the patient it will settle without arranging examination
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires arranging timely in-person otoscopy, a necrotising-OE red-flag screen, and adequate analgesia. Remote prescription of ototopical drops without TM confirmation is an error of commission.
